1998-02-01.title: Italian Ban On Paying Kidnappers Stirs Anger
1998-02-01.abstract: Recent kidnappings in Italy have increased public outrage over 1991 anti-kidnapping law, which bars families of victims from paying ransom or negotiating with kidnappers, except with cooperation of law enforcement, and imposes automatic and obligatory freeze on assets of affected families; victims families are denouncing what they regard as denial of their right to rescue their own (M)

1998-02-01.title: Ecuadoreans Want Texaco to Clear Toxic Residue
1998-02-01.abstract: Residents of Shushufindi, Ecuador, seeks more than $1 billion from Texaco Inc in compensation for health and environmental damage and for clean-up of hundreds of waste pits with toxic chemicals, which were left behind after company ceased operations there after two decades; class-action suit, dismissed in US court in 1997 but currently under appeal, is being closely watched by oil and mining industries; suggests that pliable governments, which may have been possible advantage for foreign investors in past, could actually prove liability, as citizens take on companies directly; Texaco vehemently defends its actions; map; photo (M)

1998-02-01.title: BENEATH THE TURBAN: A special report.; Mullah Who Charmed Iran Is Struggling to Change It
1998-02-01.abstract: Mohammed Khatami, Iran's new President who charmed nation during election campaign by advocating change in many of country's revolutionary policies, finds himself surrounded by extraordinary obstacles as he moves within constraints of Islamic Republic in which he believes; knows little about economy, whose direction is determined by five-year plan he inherited, and lacks political apparatus or party to give cohesion to goals; is confronted by powerful political enemies determined to deprive him of power to shape policy and to reach out to US; is less important, under country's Constitution, than Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Supreme leader of Islamic Republic who controls armed forces, judiciary and security, intelligence and broadcasting services; on many issues, both men have sharply different views; in eyes of US, Khatami has yet to prove he has power or will to curb groups or individuals engaged in activities defined s terrorism; photos (M)

1998-02-01.title: Britain to Urge U.N. to Find Iraq in Violation of Arms Terms
1998-02-01.abstract: British Foreign Secretary, Robin Cook, meets Sec of State Madeleine K Albright in London and later says that Britain and US are united in commitment to prevent Iraq from acquiring weapons of mass destruction; says Britain will seek Security Council resolution condemning Pres Saddam Hussein for breaching arms agreement; suggests resolution could be coupled with expansion of oil-for-food program, under which Iraqi oil is sold to buy food, medicine and other relief items needed by ordinary Iraqis; he and Albright say they would prefer diplomatic solution that would allow UN arms inspectors to continue their work; photo (M)

1998-02-01.title: Chile's Ex-Dictator Tries to Dictate His Future Role
1998-02-01.abstract: Gen Augusto Pinochet balks at retiring as commander-in-chief of Chile's because of lawsuit by governing Christian Democratic Party to prevent him from becoming Senator for Life; position, which would grant some immunity from prosecution, is guaranteed to former heads of state under Constitution drafted by Pinochet's Government during 17-year dictatorship, which ended in 1990 after deaths of more than 3,000 political opponents; Pinochet has threatened to use compromising intelligence from secret military files against those who would keep him from assuming Senate seat; photo (M)

1998-02-02.title: Hector Eduardo Cabanillas, 84; Kept Eva Peron's Grave a Secret
1998-02-02.abstract: Retired Argentine Gen Hector Eduardo Cabanillas, who was respononsible for military Government's successful efforts to hide body of Eva Peron after her death in 1952, dies at age 84 (M)

1998-02-02.title: Albright Rebukes Israeli and Palestinian Leaders
1998-02-02.abstract: US Sec of State Madeleine K Albright scolds Israeli and Palestinian leaders for delaying fundamental decisions necessary to revive flagging peace talks, news conference, Tel Aviv; says issues at stake are difficult and complex, but that both sides should make difficult decisions and exercise leadership necessary to secure peace; reassures Israel of swift and forceful US retaliation in event of Iraqi missile attack on Israel or its neighbors; photo (M)

1998-02-02.title: Most Asian Stocks Surge, Led by Indonesia
1998-02-02.abstract: Indonesian stocks surge to their highest point in over three months on optimism that banking reforms announced by Government will put economy back on track (S)

1998-02-02.title: Iraq Still Unpopular in One Arab Land
1998-02-02.abstract: Informal sampling of public opinion in Kuwait shows that many people still want revenge against Iraq for its seven-month military occupation in 1990-91 (M)

1998-02-02.title: Indonesia Turns Its Chinese Into Scapegoats
1998-02-02.abstract: Anti-Chinese sentiment is surfacing in Rengasdengklok and other parts of Indonesia during Asian financial crisis; Chinese make up less than 4 percent of Indonesia's 200 million people, but they hold as much as 70 percent of country's private wealth; map; photo (M)

1998-02-03.title: From a Friend Indeed, Clinton Awaits a Visit
1998-02-03.abstract: Prime Min Tony Blair of Britain, who arrives in Washington on Feb 4, says he considers Pres Clinton a friend and criticizes 'arrogant' politicians and media who he says 'try to reverse the outcome of elections'; praises Clinton's backing for British and Irish efforts to achieve settlement in Northern Ireland; photo (M)

1998-02-03.title: Anthrax Findings Fuel Worry on Vaccine
1998-02-03.abstract: Debate among Government scientists as to whether current anthrax vaccine will adequately protect US troops is rekindled after scientists at Los Alamos National Laboratory discover that anthrax spores released in 1979 accident at Soviet military site in Sverdlovsk probably contained blend of at least four different strains of anthrax bacilli, as if it had been devised to overwhelm vaccine (M)

1998-02-03.title: Havana Journal; A Sentimental Journey To La Casa of Childhood
1998-02-03.abstract: Mirta Ojito article on her return to Cuba as reporter to cover Pope John Paul II's visit; discusses her emotional return to house of her childhood in Santos Suarez; notes that she was 16 when she left Cuba with her parents during first days of Mariel boat lift in May 1980; says her home remains practically as they left it, seemingly frozen in time, like much of Cuba; map; photos (M)

1998-02-03.title: Hillary Clinton Lectures in Davos
1998-02-03.abstract: DAVOS, Switzerland—  Hillary Rodham Clinton, in a toughly worded speech here Monday, said American business leaders had neglected their own interests by failing to support "fast-track" trade negotiating power for her husband.

1998-02-03.title: Gaullists Look for a New Start but Decide Against a New Name
1998-02-03.abstract: Pres Jacques Chirac's Rally for Republic party, defeated in France's last legislative elections, vows at convention in Paris to do better next time and almost changes its name to Rally for France to get fresh start (M)

1998-02-04.title: Liberian Slayings Began Brutal Trend in Africa
1998-02-04.abstract: Slaying by guerrillas of some 1,000 people in Marshall, Liberia, in Aug 1990 emerges in retrospect as an atrocity that set pattern for decade of brutal conflicts in Africa; civil war, which killed as many as 10 percent of Liberia's 2.5 million people and made refugees of another 700,000, became prototype for other African conflicts: fighting blurred all distinctions between military and civilian targets, ethnicity was key factor in gruesome bouts of killing and children were used as soldiers; war's most disturbing legacy for Africa is notion that combat should be waged against unarmed civilians; map; photos (M)

1998-02-04.title: Jerusalem Journal; If It's a Hard Sell, Let's Try Beards and Yarmulkes
1998-02-04.abstract: Amos Ben Naeh and his unique modeling agency have recruited number of strictly Orthodox Jewish men as well as some women and children to take part in advertisements in Israel aimed both at growing Orthodox population and general population; cites stress on modesty, with men and women not appearing in same ads; models note some Orthodox people are vehemently opposed to modeling; photo (M)

1998-02-04.title: Where People Go Hungry, the Arts Are Starved, Too
1998-02-04.abstract: Intellectual life in Baghdad, Iraq, is disintegrating under seven years of economic sanctions and two decades of Pres Saddam Hussein's increasingly authoritarian rule; families are selling their books to raise money for food and paper is in such short supply that even Government publications have been cut in size, while private publication is impossible; photo (M)

1998-02-04.title: El Nino Effect to Cause More Harm in Southeast : Long Spell Of Drought Seen in Asia
1998-02-04.abstract: BANGKOK—  The drought in Southeast Asia will cause billions of dollars in damage and continue at least until March, climate experts said at a conference organized by the Asian Disaster Preparedness Center in Bangkok on Tuesday.

1998-02-04.title: U.S. Forces Ready to Strike, Pentagon Says
1998-02-04.abstract: Defense Department holds that US has more than enough firepower arrayed around Persian Gulf to cripple targets across Iraq, even if Saudi Arabia bars use of jets based on its territory; has contingency plans for striking Iraq without use of Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi desert (M)

1998-02-05.title: Challenge Is Raised to Plan for a Holocaust Monument in Berlin
1998-02-05.abstract: Nineteen prominent German intellectuals, historians and authors, including writer Gunter Grass, urge Germany to abandon plans for gigantic Holocaust memorial in heart of Berlin, letter to Chancellor Helmut Kohl and other supporters of project; hold huge abstract installation would be inappropriate (M)

1998-02-05.title: Low-Flying Jets a Common Sight in Vale of Death
1998-02-05.abstract: Residents of Cavalese, Italy, where American military jet cut ski lift cable, say they complained repeatedly about planes, mostly Italian Air Force jets, using area as training ground for low-level flying; see Prime Min Romano Prodi's visit to blame American pilot as disingenuous; map; photos (M)

1998-02-05.title: U.N. Resolutions Allow Attack on the Likes of Iraq
1998-02-05.abstract: Clinton Administration's stand that it has right to attack Iraq to force Saddam Hussein to open sites to weapons inspectors is based on succession of Security Council resolutions and statements and on United Nations Charter; American and other diplomats are pressing Security Council for new resolution declaring Iraq in 'material breach' of its commitments, move opposed by Russia (M)

1998-02-05.title: Vast India Seeks a Focus in Its Election Campaign
1998-02-05.abstract: Sonia Gandi, campaigning for Congress Party, is only figure who has shown any signs of rousing Indian electorate from widespread apathy; noncandidate draws large crowds across nation reminiscent of enthusiasm stirred by her mother-in-law, late Indira Gandhi, and her slain husband, Rajiv Gandhi; few believe she alone can restore party to dominant position it long held; polls indicate swing toward Bharatiya Janata Party, Hindu nationalist group; photo (M)

1998-02-05.title: Israel's 50th Anniversary Preparations Are Mired in Squabbling
1998-02-05.abstract: Israel's planning for its 50th anniversary celebration is in disarray, as Tourism Min Moshe Katsav resigns role in effort, citing negative stories leaked to press by Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu's office; agrees not to quit Government after Netanyahu condemns press attacks; resignation follows successive resignations of three directors of planning committee, which is mired in political, budgetary and personal squabbles; date of formal start of celebration has been changed several times and many events canceled or changed (M)

1998-02-06.title: Bad News, Silver Lining For Indonesian Laborers
1998-02-06.abstract: Indonesian security forces are preparing for social unrest as millions of people lose their jobs during nation's deepening recession; situation at steel plant in Surabaya noted; map (M)

1998-02-06.title: Cristobal Martinez-Bordiu, 75, Surgeon Who Cared for Franco
1998-02-06.abstract: Cristobal Martinez-Bordiu, surgeon who cared for Gen Francisco Franco and married Franco's only child, dies at age of 75 (M)

1998-02-06.title: Seoul Agrees on Layoffs and New Cuts in Effort to End Crisis
1998-02-06.abstract: South Korea's Pres-elect Kim Dae Jung reaches landmark agreement with business and unions that lays groundwork for layoffs throughout nation and brings country big step closer to resolving its economic crisis (M)

1998-02-06.title: Push for Peace Shows Split Over Inspections
1998-02-06.abstract: Diplomatic emissaries have descended on Baghdad in current crisis with Iraq, all of them opposed to heavy bombing that US has threatened and all of them willing to consider compromise with Saddam Hussein to prevent it; negotiations underscore vast division over UN inspections of Iraq's military sites; map (M)

1998-02-06.title: Top U.N. Arms Monitor Is Said to Call Iraqi Plans to End Crisis Inadequate
1998-02-06.abstract: United Nations chief weapons investigator Richard Butler tells Security Council that recent proposals floated by Iraq to defuse crisis over weapons inspections are not good enough (M)

1998-02-07.title: One Pilot Is Killed as 2 U.S. Marine Jets Collide Over the Gulf
1998-02-07.abstract: Two Marine Corps F-18 fighters on training mission collide over Persian Gulf, killing one of pilots; photo (S)

1998-02-07.title: U.S. Commander Sounds Alert on Indonesia Unrest
1998-02-07.abstract: SINGAPORE—  A senior U.S. commander has expressed concern that Indonesia could be on the verge of social and political instability.

1998-02-07.title: Jet in Italy Flew Too Low, A Top U.S. Official Admits
1998-02-07.abstract: Brig Gen Guy M Vander Linden, who is in charge of naval operations in southern Europe, acknowledges that jet that sheared ski lift cables in Cavalese, Italy, on Feb 3 was flying well below approved minimum altitude and that pilot, Capt Richard J Ashby, reportedly told his lawyer that he came up to cable too suddenly for evasive actions; says aircraft appears to have stayed within its assigned 10-nautical-mile-wide flight corridor although it did stray from its point-to-point flight track; marine aviators familiar with plane involved, EA-6B Prowler, dispute suggestion, attributed to Ashby, altimeter failure may have caused plane to fly too low; map (M)

1998-02-07.title: Canadians and Alaska to Halt Lawsuits
1998-02-07.abstract: Canadian salmon fishermen and state of Alaska reach tentative agreement to drop countersuits involving blockade of an Alaskan ferry in Prince Rupert, British Columbia, during summer of 1997 (S)

1998-02-07.title: The Issues Are Political More Than Legal at U.N.
1998-02-07.abstract: Existing United Nations Security Council resolutions appear to offer ample legal basis for renewed American and British military action against Iraq, but politically the two countries are on shakier ground; there is no agreement with France, Russia, China and other countries over what the Iraqis have to do to comply with Council's inspection requirement and avoid military attack; scale of contemplated bombing is far more ambitious than previous strikes; goal and outcome are uncertain (M)

1998-02-08.title: Brazil's Reformist Chief Rides a Bucking Bronco
1998-02-08.abstract: Profile of and interview with Brazilian Pres Fernando Henrique Cardoso, leftist intellectual turned champion of free market, who has made low inflation the new religion of Brazilians; Cardoso is most responsible for turning Brazil into a global player, and forcing its people and industries to compete--to varying degrees--in a modern econoy; photo (M)

1998-02-08.title: Neo-Nazis Carving Out Fiefs in Eastern Germany
1998-02-08.abstract: Survey of neo-Nazi scene in eastern Germany; sociologists, politicians and residents assert that across the former East Germany, neo-Nazi wave is building on spoiled hopes of Germany's unification, drawing as much on nostalgia for clear-cut conformism of Communist dictatorship as on equally unambiguous nationalism and racial exclusivism of Nazism; styling themselves, moreover, as freedom fighters--paradoxically in tradition of leftist guerrilla warfare--young neo-Nazis are seeking to establish what they call 'national liberated zones'; Interior Ministry statistics show that of 6,400 violence-prone neo-Nazis estimated to be in Germany, 3,700--more than half--live in eastern Germany; in first six months of 1997, police recorded 4,829 crimes committed by neo-Nazis--353 of them involving violent attacks; map (M)

1998-02-08.title: Cohen Tries To Calm Italy Over Deaths At Ski Lift
1998-02-08.abstract: Defense Sec William S Cohen seeks to contain political fallout in Italy over deaths caused when American jet clipped ski lift's cable, pledging full cooperation in joint investigation and review of all NATO training flights in Italy; despite his pledges and apologies, it is clear that divisions remain over investigation and its consequences (M)

1998-02-08.title: Economic Troubles Back Home Squeeze Asian Students in U.S.
1998-02-08.abstract: Asian students in United States colleges--from South Korea, Malaysia, Indonesia and Thailand--are feeling economic squeeze as currencies of those countries have fallen hard over several months; students see bank accounts halved, parents thrown out of work, family businesses dissolved; a number of students have merely toned down spending habits that had been fed by boom times at home; but others are struggling to stay in United States, where they have found broader range of programs in technology, business, science and the arts in a less stringent atmosphere than back home; photo (L)

1998-02-08.title: U.N.'s Long-Muddled 'Oil-for-Food' Program Makes Progress in Iraq
1998-02-08.abstract: United Nations' long-muddled 'oil-for-food' program, plan allowing Iraq to sell some oil to meet urgent needs of its people, is finally beginning to make a difference, although problems and shortfalls remain, particularly in medicine and medical equipment; lack of medicine is caused not only by sanctions but also by Govt decisions to build palaces and monuments (M)

1998-02-09.title: 'Black' Colleges Are Luring Back Their Own From the Ivy Leagues
1998-02-09.abstract: WASHINGTON—  Ivy League and other prestigious universities in the United States compete vigorously to recruit the best and the brightest of each year's crop of African-American high school graduates. Last June Malaika Jones was one of them.

1998-02-09.title: A Little English Goes Far in Seoul
1998-02-09.abstract: SEOUL—  From 7 A.M. to well into the evening, in hundreds of small classrooms across the country, young Koreans are struggling to learn English conversation.

1998-02-09.title: London Journal; Diana's Hereafter, an Eternity of Newsstand Life
1998-02-09.abstract: British tabloids are recycling news about late Princess Diana and are casting about for stand-in figures to halt flagging circulation; photo (M)

1998-02-09.title: The Many Faces of Montessori
1998-02-09.abstract: LONDON—  The word "Montessori" can produce an astonishing array of responses, from "too lax" to "too strict" to "cultish." The images range from pre-schoolers running wild to others forced to sit still with folded hands.

1998-02-09.title: Jet Crew Voices Sympathy for Ski Lift Victims' Kin
1998-02-09.abstract: Crew of US Air Force jet that shared ski lift cables in northern Italy killing all 20 passengers aboard cable car, issue message of sympathy to families of victims; US officials deny charges that delay in handing over jet's mission recorder is part of attempted cover-up (M)

1998-02-10.title: Israel's Chief Rabbis Reject Call By Non-Orthodox on Conversion
1998-02-10.abstract: Israel's Chief Rabbinate categorically rules out any cooperation with non-Orthodox branches of Judaism on conversions and religious rites, but stops short of overtly discarding proposed compromise; Reform and Conservative leaders in Israel and US say decision represents outright rejection and call it declaration of war; say they will immediately resume legal and political battles for recognition of their conversions, which they suspended during search for compromise; Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu, who has been dogged by issue since he took office and has sought to avoid confrontations between various streams of Judaism, hails decision; says it promotes national consensus among Jewish people and state of Israel (M)

1998-02-10.title: U.S. Jewish Group Sets Up Office In Berlin as Sentinel Against Bias
1998-02-10.abstract: American Jewish Committee, in move reflecting resurgence of Jewish life in Germany, opens permanent office in Berlin at site once owned by Jewish family that fled Nazis; ceremonies attended by high-level German, US and Israeli officials (M)

1998-02-10.title: World News Briefs; Jesse Jackson in Kenya In Effort to End Violence
1998-02-10.abstract: Rev Jesse Jackson visits Kenya for second time in three months as Pres Clinton's special envoy in effort to defuse ethnic tensions and keep democratic reforms on track (S)

1998-02-10.title: Police-Kidnapper Links Seen in Mexico
1998-02-10.abstract: Arrest of three anti-kidnapping agents from police force in Morelos state, Mex, including chief of its anti-kidnapping squad, lends credence to allegations by businessmen and human rights activists that have been long scoffed at by authorities, that police have been protecting kidnapping gangs terrorizing area south of Mexico City; map (M)

1998-02-10.title: U.N. Chief Cancels Trip to Mideast as a Hunt for Compromise Continues
1998-02-10.abstract: UN Sec Gen Kofi Annan cancels long-planned trip to Middle East to help work toward peaceful solution of Iraqi crisis as diplomatic efforts intensify; efforts of Russia, France and Arab League to find formula acceptable to Iraq as well as Security Council discussed (M)

1998-02-11.title: Ban on Protests In West Bank
1998-02-11.abstract: Palestinian police ban pro-Iraqi demonstrations on West Bank and Gaza Strip, which have turned into anti-American displays and clashes with Israeli soldiers; Palestinian Authority fears demonstrations could raise tensions with Israel and harm ties with United States (S)

1998-02-11.title: No Need to Humiliate Iraq, The Secretary General Says
1998-02-11.abstract: United Nations Sec Gen Kofi Annan, urging flexibility in finding peaceful solution to Iraqi crisis, holds UN should not insist on humiliating Pres Saddam Hussein; sounds his strongest reservations yet about military option; Britain circulates document about Iraqi chemical weapon not previously mentioned publicly by UN inspectors (M)

1998-02-11.title: Inquiry on Ski-Lift Deaths Leads Marines to Relieve a Colonel
1998-02-11.abstract: Marine Corp relieves Lt Col Stephen L Watters of command of squadron of jets like one that severed cables of Cavalese, Italy, ski lift because he told crew members to destroy potential evidence in probe; probe has broadened to include his squadron, which was not involved in incident, because it rotates with squadron involved; at issue is whether crews break altitude rules for training flights (M)

1998-02-11.title: Edginess Becomes a Way of Life for Indonesians
1998-02-11.abstract: Fear of political instability spurred by economic crisis has brought more Indonesian troops into streets, with Surabaya, nation's second-largest city, on full alert; residents, despite outward nonchalance, are nervous about reports of latest riots in smaller towns; only major eruption in Jakarta is seen likely to have big impact on nation's stability; map; photo (M)

1998-02-11.title: Bombing Gas Sites May Pose Risk of Gulf War Syndrome
1998-02-11.abstract: Some members of White House panel of experts on illnesses of Persian Gulf war veterans, noting United States preparations to bomb Iraqi poison gas sites, cite possible link between veterans' illnesses and exposure to Iraqi poison gas, letter to Pres Clinton; Prof Marguerite Knox, letter's main author, disputes recent news reports suggestings illnesses result soley from wartime stress; Prof Arthur L Caplan, letter signer, warns American troops are still not adequately defended against chemical or biological arms (M)

1998-02-12.title: World News Briefs; Britain and Ireland Weigh Sinn Fein Ouster
1998-02-12.abstract: Irish Republic and Britain say they will soon decide whether to expel Sinn Fein, Irish Republican Army's political wing, from Northern Ireland peace talks because of new violence attributed to IRA; Irish Prime Min Bertie Ahern and British Min Mo Mowlam comment (S)

1998-02-12.title: A Growing Optimism on Ending Graft in Mexico
1998-02-12.abstract: Mexican Pres Ernesto Zedillo expresses confidence that war on drugs is succeeding, interview; cites unprecedented drive against narcotics corruption in army; holds United States process of annually certifying anti-drug efforts of scores of nations is probably detrimental to war on drugs; discusses political reform; outlines what seems to be hardening approach to Zapatista guerrillas in Chiapas state; discusses nation's economic recovery; cites concern over mounting crime; photo (M)

1998-02-12.title: Jakarta's Plan To Peg Rupiah Powers a Rally Across Region
1998-02-12.abstract: BANGKOK—  Despite the opposition of the World Bank and the doubts of many economists, Indonesia confirmed Wednesday that it intended to stabilize its battered currency by pegging it at a fixed rate.

1998-02-12.title: On U.S. Carrier, Not Just a Routine Day
1998-02-12.abstract: Defense Sec William S Cohen visits aircraft carrier USS George Washington, which polices 'no flight' zone over southern Iraq; says he hopes American military buildup in area will result in peaceful end to confrontation with Iraq; photo (M)

1998-02-12.title: A Possible Russian Link to Iraq Arms Buildup
1998-02-12.abstract: United Nations weapons inspectors reportedly have found 1995 document indicating Russia discussed selling Iraq equipment that could be used to develop biological weapons; Iraq offers to open for limited time small number of sites it has closed to arms inspectors; United States and Britain continue to insist on full access to all sites (M)

1998-02-13.title: Brazil Congress Votes to Scale Down Social Security Benefits
1998-02-13.abstract: Brazilian lawmakers approve bills to strip down social security benefits and job protections; measures are cornerstone of Govt's four-year plan to stabilize economy (M)

1998-02-13.title: American Arms vs. Iraq
1998-02-13.abstract: Military analysis of possible United States confrontation with Iraq; America's vast weaponry is seen insufficent to accomplish what Pres Clinton has stated as goal of military action: to 'substantially reduce or delay' Iraq's ability to develop and deliver weapons of mass destruction; senior American military officials say there is wide gap between political aims administration has set forth and military ability to achieve them (M)

1998-02-13.title: Russia Arrests Ex-Colonel In '94 Killing Of Journalist
1998-02-13.abstract: Russian officials announce they have arrested retired military intelligence official, Pavel Popovskikh, in connection with killing of investigative journalist three years ago; journalist, Dmitri Kholodov, had been investigating corruption in Russian military when he was killed by opening booby-trapped suitcase (M)

1998-02-13.title: 1948-98: 50-Year Palestinian 'Catastrophe'
1998-02-13.abstract: Palestinians, one day after Israel began celebrating its 50th year of independence, gather in Ramallah, West Bank, to plan different sort of commemoration: 50th anniversary of what they call 'the catastrophe'; nakbah, which also means calamity or disaster, is Arabic word that Palestinians use to describe their defeat and exodus in 1948 Arab-Israeli war that followed Israel's declaration of independence; at conference, organized by Palestinian Authority's ministry of culture, politicians, intellectuals, educators and community leaders gather to plan year of activities memorializing their loss; photo of Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat (M)

1998-02-13.title: For a Shaky Japanese Economy, The Premier Is a Hesitant Steward
1998-02-13.abstract: Japanese Prime Min Ryutaro Hashimoto's uncertain stewardship of his country's economy discussed; officials in Washington and Asian capitals are increasingly alarmed and angered by Hashimoto's reluctance to stimulate fragile Japanese economy; they fear that his inaction could worsen Asian financial crisis and provoke global slowdown; they foresee economic confrontation about international repercussions of Japan's domestic economic policy, rather than about trade barriers; photo (M)

1998-02-14.title: Technical Team Is Sent to Iraq To Try to Define Off-Limits Sites
1998-02-14.abstract: Sec Gen Kofi Annan sends technical team to Iraq to clear up confusion over so-called presidential sites that Saddam Hussein has declared off-limits to United Nations inspectors (M)

1998-02-14.title: Another Rebuff to Iraq
1998-02-14.abstract: Pres Clinton and top aides begin preparing nation for war in Iraq and for grim prospect of civilian casualties on the ground and of American pilots shot down from the skies; Clinton says he would not be deterred by Russia's opposition to an attack on Iraq if Pres Saddam Hussein does not allow United Nations weapons inspectors unfettered access; photo of memorial in Baghdad to people killed by American bombs in an attack on suburb of Amiriya on Feb 13, 1991 (M)

1998-02-14.title: Russia's Stake In Iraq Bolsters Anti-War Cry
1998-02-14.abstract: Pres Boris Yeltsin's opposition to American military action against Iraq is shared by Russia's political and business elite and the average Russian and reflects potent combination of foreign policy and commercial interests; economic ties include $3.8 billion agreement signed by Lukoil, Russia's premier oil company, to develop Iraq's Qurna oilfield once economic sanctions are removed; many Russians still find it hard to accept that US has the power, will and resources to launch a military campaign in the face of determined Russian opposition; however, there is no serious talk of providing military assistance to Baghdad or desire for renewing cold war hostilities with US; Russia also has a generally satisfactory record of observing economic sanctions against Iraq; graph of Russian opinion poll on conflict (M)

1998-02-14.title: Gene-Engineered Anthrax: Is It a Weapon?
1998-02-14.abstract: American scientists will seek to determine whether new form of anthrax created by Russians through genetic engineering is merely theoretical or a practical weapon and whether it is able to defeat the vaccine American troops will soon get to protect them from anthrax (M)

1998-02-14.title: French Police Rule Out Lead in Diana's Death
1998-02-14.abstract: French police rule out any connection between car crash that killed Diana, Princess of Wales, and a white Fiat once owned by a celebrity photographer (M)

1998-02-15.title: Using Gifts as Bait, Peru Sterilizes Poor Women
1998-02-15.abstract: Peru, in what some call ambitious family planning program run amok, reportedly pressures poor women to undergo sterilization operations; program has led to deaths of at least two women and injured hundreds; critics say health workers, hurrying to meet quotas, are not telling women about alternative methods of contraception or that sterilization is usually irreversible; say many state doctors perform sloppy operations, at times in unsanitary conditions; photo (M)

1998-02-15.title: Now Murder Is Suggested In the Death Of Diana
1998-02-15.abstract: Mohamed al-Fayed, whose son, Dodi, died in Paris car crash with Diana, Princess of Wales, backs theory that couple was murdered, newspaper interview; her family and Buckingham Palace score stand (M)

1998-02-15.title: Bomb Blasts at Campaign Rally in India Kill 30
1998-02-15.abstract: India's election campaign is rocked by series of bomb explosions that kill at least 30 peoplein southern city of Coimbatore as thousands gather for rally by Hindu nationalist leader Lal Kishinchand Advandi (S)a

1998-02-15.title: Threats Worry 3 Who Tied Croatian Army to Atrocities
1998-02-15.abstract: Three former Croatian soldiers who testified before war crimes tribunal say they have been repeatedly subjected to beatings and death threats; Zdenko Bando, Milan Levar and Zdenko Ropac say that hundreds of ethnic Serbs, as well as Croats who opposed the nationalist movement, were executed and buried in mass graves around town of Gospic by the Croation Army, paramilitary groups and police; say documents they turned over to The Hague implicate senior Croatian officals, including Defense Min Gojko Susak; photo (M)

1998-02-15.title: Elephants Are Friends, China Tells Its Farmers
1998-02-15.abstract: Local officials in southern China, after years of neglect, are getting serious about protecting elephants and other wildlife, punishing poachers severely; say elephant population has stabilized at over 200 (M)

1998-02-16.title: Soweto Journal; Horseplay It's Not: Blacks Dream of Finishing First
1998-02-16.abstract: Enos Mafokate, 52, barred by apartheid from becoming show jumper, runs only riding academy for young blacks in South Africa, Soweto; photos (M)

1998-02-16.title: HINDU-FIRST PARTY EXPECTED TO GAIN IN INDIA'S ELECTION
1998-02-16.abstract: India's Hindu nationalist party, led by Atal Bihari Vajpayee, is expected to make major gains in voting set to begin across India ; expectation that party may do well enough to form government, or at least head governing coalition, is disturbing to many Muslims and to other Indians who reject Hindu nationalism; anxieties have been growing, with troops moved into some areas and curfews set to bar election violence; Sonia Gandhi, Italian-born Roman Catholic who pits her soaring personal popularity against tarnished image of her Congress Party, poses strongest challenge to nationalists; map; photo (L)

1998-02-16.title: The Blue Nun Gets a Makeover/ Call Her 'Fruity,' Not Sweet : The World's 'Oldest Wine Brand' Goes Upscale
1998-02-16.abstract: MAINZ, Germany—  Just when some upscale winegrowers in Germany hoped she had gone away, the Blue Nun has returned.

1998-02-16.title: Turkey Charges Leading Politicians as Threat to Nation's Unity
1998-02-16.abstract: Turkish prosecutors again signal determination to restrict speech that threatens national unity; indict Istanbul Mayor Reccep Tayip Erdogan on charges leader of Islamic-oriented party called on his supporters to form holy war; also order arrest of entire 57-member directorate of only legal Kurdish-based political party; cite party calendar that includes picture of jailed Kurdish leader Leyla Zana and list of Kurds and other victims of 'mystery killings' (M)

1998-02-16.title: Time to Bomb: Olympics Bad, No Moon Good
1998-02-16.abstract: United States, in scheduling possible bombing attack on Iraq, considers wide range of factors, including intricacies of Muslim religious calendar, dates for moonless nights and Pres Clinton's travel plans; awaits political judgment on diplomatic efforts to end impasse; photo (M)

1998-02-17.title: The G.O.P. Is Urging Hussein's Ouster
1998-02-17.abstract: Republican-dominated chorus in Congress and in foreign-policy circles is demanding long-term strategy to oust Iraqi Pres Saddam Hussein; options include enlarging existing no-fly and no-drive zones, in Iraq, tightening economic embargo, disbursing billions in frozen Iraqi assets to provisional Government and setting up 'Radio Free Iraq' to stir public opposition and arming; photo (M)

1998-02-17.title: Canada Seeks Legal Advice On the Status Of Quebec
1998-02-17.abstract: Canadian Government mounts legal challenge to right of Quebec Province to secede without agreement of rest of country; lawyers argue before Supreme Court, asserting that unilateral secession would go both against Constitution and international law; tough stance seeks to end most divisive issue and end uncertainty that has dangled over country for more than 30 years (M)

1998-02-17.title: Over 200 Die as Taiwan Jet Crashes in Bad Weather
1998-02-17.abstract: China Airlines Airbus jumbo jet, on flight from Bali to Tapiei, crashes during landing in fog and rain at Taipei's international airport killing 197 passengers and crew members, including Taiwan's central bank governor Sheu Yuandong and five Americans; photo; map (M)

1998-02-17.title: Britain Takes Step Toward Ousting I.R.A. Ally at Ulster Talks
1998-02-17.abstract: British Government moves closer to temporarily expelling Sinn Fein, political wing of Irish Republican Army, from Northern Ireland peace talks because of involvement in killing of civilian week ago in Belfast (M)

1998-02-17.title: Indonesia Edges Toward a Showdown With IMF
1998-02-17.abstract: SINGAPORE—  A confrontation between Indonesia and its main creditors moved closer Monday as Indonesian officials said the government would press ahead with a plan aimed at stabilizing its currency despite opposition to its strategy from the International Monetary Fund, the World Bank, the United States and the European Union.

1998-02-18.title: Egypt's Leader Warns of Anti-U.S. Ire
1998-02-18.abstract: Anti-American protests break out at Cairo university as top Egyptian officials warn United States policy toward Iraq undermines America's standing in Mideast (M)

1998-02-18.title: Jordan Is Angered by Israeli Findings on Assassination Fiasco
1998-02-18.abstract: Jordanians react angrily to Israeli commission report absolving Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu of blame in botched Sept effort by Israeli secret service to assassinate Islamic militant leader Khaled Meshal in Amman; senior official says suspension of security cooperation ordered by King Hussein after incident may be extended as long as Danny Yatom remains head of Israel's secret service (M)

1998-02-18.title: I.R.A. Ally Asks Irish Court to Block Ouster From Peace Talks
1998-02-18.abstract: Sinn Fein, Irish Republican Army's political wing, says it will ask Irish Republic court to block any move to expel it from Northern Ireland peace talks; Irish Prime Min Bertie Ahern rejects Sinn Fein appeal to break with British on expulsion issue, holding there is reason to believe IRA was involved in two recent killings (M)

1998-02-18.title: Sudan to Loosen Islamic Rule a Bit
1998-02-18.abstract: Ruling Islamic party that has molded Sudan into religious state is enacting new constitution officals say will allow return to party politics, banned since 1989, and greater political freedom; 6,500-member National Congress meeting, Khartoum, debates and adopts constitutional changes that seek to wed principles of Islamic state with free speech and party politics (M)

1998-02-18.title: CLINTON DESCRIBES GOALS FOR A STRIKE ON IRAQI ARSENALS
1998-02-18.abstract: Pres Clinton, charging Iraqi Pres Saddam Hussein lies and cheats to cling to chemical and biological arms, says he is prepared to order air strikes to 'seriously diminish' Iraqi threat to region and world, televised speech at Pentagon; seeks to rally public support for possible action; repeatedly says he hopes diplomats can persuade Hussein to comply with weapons inspections; says force cannot eliminate Iraq's ability to make such arms; appears with Defense Sec William S Cohen, Vice Pres Al Gore and Gen Henry Hugh Shelton after military briefing by Joint Chiefs of Staff; photo (M)

1998-02-19.title: Among Blacks, Washington Ranks as the Top Surname
1998-02-19.abstract: When Gregory Washington met Craig Washington, he saw something he had never observed in someone with his last name: white skin. "I thought he was going to be black," said Gregory, an African-American, of his first meeting with a new co-worker at a company in Torrance, California. "I told him he was the first white Washington I'd ever seen. I joked that we must be brothers."

1998-02-19.title: Suharto's Choice as No. 2: Whiz or Loose Cannon?
1998-02-19.abstract: B J Habibie, Indonesian Pres Suharto's evident choice for Vice President and potential successor, is technological whiz, political naif and economic eccentric viewed by many as loose cannon; military, despite longstanding antagonism toward him, endorses him after he is nominated by all three political parties; he lacks strong power base beyond Suharto and may be unable to retain power on own; photo (M)

1998-02-19.title: Johannesburg Journal; Of Leaders and Lovers: A South African Epidemic
1998-02-19.abstract: Love lives of South Africa's president and former presidents are making headlines; Pres Nelson Mandela, 79, is involved with Graca Machel, widow of late Mozambique president; P W Botha, 82 and recently widowed, is engaged; F W de Klerk, 61, is divorcing wife to wed Elita Georgiadis, wife of Greek shipping tycoon, spurring speculation that his recent resignation from politics was prompted by affair; his National Party has little tolerance for such behavior; photos (M)

1998-02-19.title: TOP CLINTON AIDES FIND DOUBT ON IRAQ AT CAMPUS IN OHIO
1998-02-19.abstract: State Sec Madeleine K Albright, Defense Sec William S Cohen and national security adviser Samuel R Berger take part in unruly public forum, Ohio State University, Columbus, in bid for public support for attack on Iraq; face tough questions, catcalls and concern about American goals; chanting by some 200 anti-war protesters sometimes drowns out officials; forum is televised live around world (M)

1998-02-19.title: 'Dinosaur' Stirs Mexico's Old Guard
1998-02-19.abstract: Gov Manuel Bartlett Diaz's outlines plan to seek ruling party's presidential nomination, interview; scores party leaders as timid bureacrats unwilling to stand up to opponents; bid to wrest control of troubled party from foreign-trained economists and technocrats and return it to old guard stirs enthusiasm in party and undermines Pres Ernesto Zedillo's authority; map; photo (M)

1998-02-20.title: A Rising Hindu Tide Frightens India's Muslims
1998-02-20.abstract: India's muslims feel anxious about rising tide of Hinduism in their country; since 1947, when partioning of British-ruled Inida set off vast migration of Muslims and Hindus across frontiers of newly independent nations of India and Pakistan, there have been few moments when India's 120 million Muslims have felt so vulnerable; Muslims' current anxiety is that when election results are known, about two weeks hence, a new govt will be formed by Hindu nationalist group, Bharatiya Janata Party, which has maintained wide lead over other parties; for India's Muslims, a Hindu nationalist govt would come to power with legacy many find frightening; there is no escaping what Hindu hard-liners intend--that India should abandon sense of itself as mosaic of different faiths, and establish primacy of Hindu majority, now 700 million in population of 980 million; photo (L)

1998-02-20.title: France Gives Strong Support To U.N. Chief
1998-02-20.abstract: Sec Gen Kofi Annan, who is traveling to Iraq, stops off in Paris and gets firm backing from French Pres Jacques Chirac for his last-ditch effort to bring peaceful resolution to crisis over weapons inspections (S)

1998-02-20.title: Swiss Reject Claim Over 2 Jews Deported in '42
1998-02-20.abstract: Switzerland's Cabinet rejects Jewish man's claim for financial damages for wrongful deportation of his parents, who were killed at Auschwitz; in first case of its kind in Switzerland, Charles Sonabend, 67, asked Swiss Govt for maximum $68,000, or $34,000 per victim, allowed under law; Govt turns away his claim, maintaining it was filed too late and not justified (S)

1998-02-20.title: CLINTON SETS OUT TO REVIVE SUPPORT FOR STAND ON IRAQ
1998-02-20.abstract: Pres Clinton, day after American policy toward Iraq was picked apart at town hall meeting at Ohio State University, tries to reassemble pieces, declaring that country would ultimately support an American military assault; declares 'If it's necessary for us to act, I believe that America will do what it always does. I Believe it will unite'; asserts that UN Sec Gen Kofi Annan's mission to Baghdad is 'a critical opportunity to achieve the outcome that all of would prefer: a peaceful and principled end to this crisis'; says he has asked Vice Pres Gore to postpone trip to South Africa next week to remain in Washington; Clinton photo (M)

1998-02-20.title: Arafat Shuts Stations Over Iraq Support
1998-02-20.abstract: Palestinian Authority shuts down nine radio and television stations in West Bank in attempt to stifle public expressions of pro-Iraqi sentiment among Palestinians; move is intended to avoid raising tensions with United States and Israel (S)

1998-02-21.title: U.N. Team Calls Iraq Sites Smaller Than Thought
1998-02-21.abstract: UN team commissioned by Sec Gen Kofi Annan to map Iraq's 'sensitive sites' concludes after three days that total area is 12.2 square miles, including 3.9 square miles covered by lakes; says many of buildings are guest houses and only one is palace; UN special commission to disarm Iraq has estimated that over 27 miles and 1,500 buildings are in off-limits sites; UN official charges Iraqi shell game to divide commission and UN; commission chairman Richard Butler, who has not been included in Annan's Baghdad talks, says issue is access, not size of area (S)

1998-02-21.title: U.N. Expands Quota for Iraq's Oil Sales
1998-02-21.abstract: UN Security Council more than doubles amount of oil that Iraq will be allowed to sell to buy food, medicine and other essential goods; approves Sec Gen Kofi Annan's proposal to allow $5.2 billion in sales every six months (S)

1998-02-21.title: Great Brington Journal; There'll Always Be a Shrine to Diana, but Must It Be Here?
1998-02-21.abstract: Great Brington Journal about great concern in small English village about huge crowds expected to visit Althorp, family home of late Princess Diana; photos; map; Earl Spencer, Diana's brother, proposes to convert stable complex into museum, set uip cafe, gift shop and 500-car parking lot in village that has 200 inhabitants and no traffic lights; Earl, who lives mostly in South Africa, also plans open-air rock concert in Diana's memory and alienates neighbors on other side of his 5,800-acre estate by proposing to sell 400 acres of woodland to developers (M)

1998-02-21.title: Pentagon Report Plays Down Cost of Expanding NATO
1998-02-21.abstract: Clinton Administration officials say new Pentagon report has found that direct costs of absorbing Poland, Hungary and Czech Republic into NATO will be $1.5 billion over ten years, much less than earlier estimates; reason is said to be that countries' defense infrastructures are in better condition than believed; critics of NATO enlargement charge manipulation of figures to placate Senators before crucial vote (S)

1998-02-21.title: Fernando Abril Martorell, 61, Key Spanish Aide
1998-02-21.abstract: Fernando Abril Martorell, former Deputy Prime Minister and a key figure in Spain's transition to democracy after Franco dictatorship, dies at 61 (S)

1998-02-22.title: U.S. Is Advising Its Citizens to Leave Iraq as Soon as Possible
1998-02-22.abstract: US advises American citizens to leave Iraq as soon as possible; Pres Clinton meets with his top foreign policy aides to discuss their options once United Nations Sec Gen Kofi Annan completes his trip to Baghdad; photo (M)

1998-02-22.title: Pope Elevates 20 Cardinals, Who Include 2 Americans
1998-02-22.abstract: Pope John Paul II elevates 20 prelates to rank of cardinal; Pope receives 19 of them during outdoor ceremony in Rome; one cardinal is hospitalized; Pope also names two other men cardinals 'in pectore,' procedure to keep their identities secret; Francis Cardinal George of Chicago and James Cardinal Stafford, former archbishop of Denver, are among new cardinals (M)

1998-02-22.title: C.I.A. Bares Its Bungling in Report on Bay of Pigs Invasion
1998-02-22.abstract: Central Intelligence Agency's report on 1961 Bay of Pigs invasion, one of most secret documents of cold war, is released to National Security Archive under Freedom of Information Act; report lays blame for disastrous invasion of Cuba on agency's own institutional arrogance, ignorance and incompetence; document also cautions those who would use CIA to overthrow enemies, saying that job belongs to Pentagon and its broad arsenal of military forces; report was written by then-CIA Inspector Gen Lyman Kirkpatrick; photo (M)

1998-02-22.title: The C.I.A. on the C.I.A.: Scathing View of Invasion
1998-02-22.abstract: Excerpts from then-CIA Inspector Gen Lyman Kirkpatrick's report on 1961 Bay of Pigs invasion (M)

1998-02-22.title: C.I.A.'s Self-Critique In Bay of Pigs Fiasco
1998-02-22.abstract: One of the most secret documents of the cold war is out, 36 years after it was written. It is the Central Intelligence Agency's own inquiry into the Bay of Pigs fiasco, the invasion to overthrow Fidel Castro that caused the deaths of nearly 1,500 raiders. With brutal honesty, the report put the blame squarely on what it described as arrogance, ignorance and incompetence within the C.I.A. itself.    The report painted a picture of an agency shot through with deadly self-deception, and concluded that its secret operations were ''ludicrous or tragic, or both.'' The document also warned those who would use the C.I.A. to overthrow enemies that such efforts belong to the Pentagon.

1998-02-23.title: Despite Threat of Missiles, the Iraqi Capital's People Take a Crisis in Stride
1998-02-23.abstract: Residents of Iraq's capital of Baghdad seem placid, cool and almost nonchalant despite possibility of military action by US because of Government's refusal to allow UN inspectors unfettered access to suspected weapons sites; photo (M)

1998-02-23.title: Turmoil in India's Largest State Clouds Vote
1998-02-23.abstract: India's general election progresses through second of three crucial days, with 200 million voters going to polls, but with results still clouded because of political turmoil in Uttar Pradesh, country's largest and most populous state with biggest electoral prize; public opinion polls suggest victory by either coalition Government led by Hindu nationalist group, or rival coalition of so-called secular parties opposed to Hindu nationalism; many political observers say election is most confused since Indira Gandhi called election in 1977 after 20-month period of emergency rule; map; photo (M)

1998-02-23.title: Accord Is Reported to Open Eight Iraqi Sites
1998-02-23.abstract: Accord reached between UN Sec Gen Kofi Annan and Iraqi Pres Saddam Hussein reportedly will allow inspections of eight presidential sites previously off limits, including variety of buildings and grounds enclosed in three compounds in Baghdad, as well as around Tikrit, Mosul and Basra; apparently does not address issue of other presidential properties, excluded from eight listed by Baghdad, identified by inspectors as suspect weapons areas or places where crucial documents may be hidden; Iraq has reportedly been persuaded to drop time limit of 60 days it wanted to impose on inspections of presidential sites (M)

1998-02-23.title: New Attacks Will Not Halt Irish Talks
1998-02-23.abstract: British and Irish Governments say terrorist attacks by splinter groups oppposed to Northern Ireland peace talks will not be allowed to derail negotiations; Gerry Adams, leader of Sinn Fein, Irish Republican Army's political wing which has been temporarily suspended because of links to recent killings of civilians, says party will not be deterred by Republican militants opposed to group's participation in talks (M)

1998-02-23.title: IRAQI AGREES TO INSPECTIONS IN A DEAL WITH U.N. LEADER; WASHINGTON AWAITS DETAILS
1998-02-23.abstract: Pres Saddam Hussein of Iraq reportedly agrees to lift ban on arms inspections at presidential properties by UN personnel, possibly defusing crisis that brought Iraq to brink of military showdown with US; agreement is reached after three-hour meeting between Hussein and UN Sec Gen Kofi Annan; Iraq reportedly agrees to give up most of demands, including time limit on inspections of presidental sites; accord reportedly changes some of methods used in inspecting presidential sites, but apparently does not address issue of other presidential properties excluded from eight listed by Baghdad--including those identified by UN as suspected weapons areas or places where crucial documents may be hidden; UN officials are hopeful it will meet Washington's requirements; photo (M)

1998-02-24.title: Q&A / Juwono Sudarsono, Defense Official : Racism in Indonesia Undercuts Unity
1998-02-24.abstract: The 7 million ethnic Chinese in Indonesia make up just 3 percent of the population, but they control a large share of economic wealth. On a visit to Singapore, Juwono Sudarsono, deputy governor of the National Resilience Institute, an arm of the Indonesian Defense Ministry that trains senior civilian and military officials, discussed the situation of the Chinese minority with Michael Richardson of the International Herald Tribune.

1998-02-24.title: A Testy Hussein Deputy and a Candid U.N. Chief Fence With the Press
1998-02-24.abstract: Deputy Prime Min Tariq Aziz of Iraq and United Nations Sec Gen Kofi Annan face the press in Baghdad and questions about what is left unanswered by agreement granting weapons inspectors unfettered access across Iraq; Aziz is testy, and Annan is very much the statesman; photo (M)

1998-02-24.title: Europeans Offer Praise For Accord With Iraq
1998-02-24.abstract: European allies warmly hail accord granting United Nations weapons inspection full access to Iraqi sites; Pres Jacques Chirac of France speaks with Pres Clinton; Prime Min Tony Blair says Britain will push for Security Council resolution authorizing military response if Pres Saddam Hussein fails to keep word; foreign ministers of European Union offer praise; photos (M)

1998-02-24.title: In the Words of Clinton: 'What Really Matters Is Iraq's Compliance'
1998-02-24.abstract: Transcript of Pres Clinton's news conference on agreement between United Nations and Iraq over access by weapons inspectors; photo (M)

1998-02-24.title: A Buildup For Kuwait Followed By Letdown
1998-02-24.abstract: Many Kuwaitis express mixed feelings about agreement between United Nations and Iraq granting weapons inspectors unfettered access to Iraqi sites; say agreement probably averts deaths of many fellow Arabs, but also leaves in power Iraqi leader whose forces invaded and occupied their country eight years ago (M)

1998-02-25.title: South Korea's New President Appeals to North to End Decades of Division
1998-02-25.abstract: Kim Dae Jung, one of Asia's most vigorous voices for democracy and political tolerance, is inaugurated as South Korea's President and immediately calls for reconciliation and cooperation with North Korea; he also calls for far-reaching changes in South Korea's economy and political structure to pull nation out of its financial crisis; photo (M)

1998-02-25.title: Rio Journal; The Beauty of It! Face Lifts Pay for Poor's Surgery
1998-02-25.abstract: Brazil's obsession with beauty has made plastic surgery popular in Rio de Janeiro, where many plastic surgeons perform expensive cosmetic surgery on wealthy patients and orthopedic surgery on poor free of charge; photo (M)

1998-02-25.title: Soviet Defector Warns of Biological Weapons
1998-02-25.abstract: Dr Kanatjan Alibekov, defector from former Soviet biological weapons program who is now known as Ken Alibek, says that Moscow's cold war plans for World War III included preparing 'hundreds of tons' of anthrax bacteria and scores of tons of smallpox and plague viruses; Alibek was second-in-command of branch of Soviet program and defected to US in 1992; he says Russian military was still running biological weapons program in 1991, year after Mikhail S Gorbachev ordered it halted; has written highly classified study of Soviet biological weapons program for US Government; says he believes a vestige of Moscow's cold war biological-weapons program is continuing in Russia under guise of defensive research; photo (M)

1998-02-25.title: Blair Puts His Weight Behind Dome Of Millennium
1998-02-25.abstract: Prime Min Tony Blair says Millennium Dome, Britain's ambitious monument to next century which is scheduled to open on Dec 31, 1999, will be built on time and on budget; dome has been derided as extravagant irrelevancy, a political vanity project and colossal waste of $1.24 billion; it was designed by architect Richard Rogers and will be built in Greenwich to mark place where Greenwich Mean Time was introduced in 1884 and where new century will officially dawn (M)

1998-02-25.title: Lawmakers Demand Prompt Test of Hussein's Promise to U.N.
1998-02-25.abstract: Republican and Democratic lawmakers make demands for quick test of Pres Saddam Hussein's promise to allow United Nations weapons inspectors unfettered and unconditional access throughout Iraq; some senators remain deeply skeptical of accord reached by United Nations Sec Gen Kofi Annan and Hussein; growing number of legislators express guarded optimism as they are briefed by senior officials of Clinton Administration; House Speaker Newt Gingrich says he 'strongly supports' initiative Clinton has taken (M)

1998-02-26.title: ASEAN Seeks Funds to Avert Another Costly Season of Haze
1998-02-26.abstract: SINGAPORE—  With forest fires in Indonesia threatening to cast another pall of smoke-borne pollution over Southeast Asia, officials in the region appealed Wednesday for international aid to help prevent a recurrence of the 1997 crisis that is estimated to have cost more than $1.3 billion in damage to health, disrupted industry and transport and lost tourism.

1998-02-26.title: Demand Is Constant/An 'Excessive Fondness' : For Indians, Ignoring World, All That Glitters Is Gold
1998-02-26.abstract: NEW DELHI—  Never mind that gold is losing its glitter as an investment worldwide, Subhash Bhola's jewelry shop is doing brisk business. So are hundreds of other gold stores that cram the same middle-class neighborhood in New Delhi.

1998-02-26.title: Not a Crook? Voters in India Don't Always Care
1998-02-26.abstract: Dharam Pal Yadav, gangster from Sambhal, India, who is accused of murder, is running for re-election to Parliament; dominant issue in campaign is criminalization of politics as symbolized by Yadav; map; photo (M)

1998-02-26.title: THE DEAL ON IRAQ: SECRET ARSENAL: The Hunt for the Germs of War -- A special report.; Iraq's Deadliest Arms: Puzzles Breed Fears
1998-02-26.abstract: William J Broad and Judith Miller special report, The Hunt for the Germs of War, discusses seven-year hunt by United Nations weapons inspectors for secret biological weapons in Iraq; notes that in 1995 Iraq grudgingly admitted that it had made enough deadly microbes to kill all people on earth; says despite progress in penetrating Iraqi secrecy, UN inspectors remain uncertain about most of Pres Saddam Hussein's facilities to wage biological warfare; map; photos (M)

1998-02-26.title: Under Close Scrutiny, Turkey's Pro-Islam Party Has a Makeover
1998-02-26.abstract: Leaders of Turkey's banned pro-Islamic party are trying to reorganize themselves in another party, but federal prosecutors and their own former leader, Necmettin Erbakan, are watching them suspiciously (M)

1998-02-27.title: Chirac Wary of Quick Attack If Iraq Breaks Inspection Deal
1998-02-27.abstract: French Pres Jacques Chirac, his popularity soaring in public opinion polls because of credit given to French diplomacy in avoiding an attack on Iraq, says in interview that France would not support calls for automatic military response if Iraq fails to live up to its latest agreement on weapons inspections (M)

1998-02-27.title: World News Briefs; Ex-Official in Spain Gets Corruption Sentence
1998-02-27.abstract: Luis Roldan, man who was chief of Spanish Civil Guard in Socialist Govt of Felipe Gonzalez, is sentenced to 28 years for embezzling millions of dollars in public funds, taking kickbacks and evading taxes; Roldan is among best known of a dozen senior political and financial figures who faced corruption charges after late 1980's economic boom (S)

1998-02-27.title: The Man Likely to Be Mexico's No. 1 Trafficker (the Job's Open)
1998-02-27.abstract: United States and Mexican authorities monitor drug trafficking scene in Mexico for signs of successor to Amado Carrillo Fuentes, who was Mexico's most influential drug trafficker, and who died during surgery last year; US official says that Rafael Munoz Talavera, long-time smuggler with links to same cartel, had emerged as likely heir (M)

1998-02-27.title: Yeltsin Gives His Cabinet Public Rebuke On Economy
1998-02-27.abstract: Russia Pres Boris N Yeltsin, in special Govt meeting that is half encounter group and half staged show trial, lashes out at his Govt for failing to correct country's ailing economy and threatens Cabinet shakeup (M)

1998-02-27.title: As Iraqi Tension Eases, Arabs Criticize U.S. Role
1998-02-27.abstract: Assessment of question of whether United States' standing in Middle East has suffered, with Iraq crisis now in remission; some diplomats and experts argue that crisis demonstrated that there is still no viable alternative to American power in region, or to American mediation between Israel and Palestinians; many Arabs and some Israeli commentators attribute outpouring of support for Iraq on Arab streets to popular perception that United States maintains double standard in Middle East (M)

1998-02-28.title: American With Cure-All Enlivens Jakarta Crisis
1998-02-28.abstract: Indonesia's dubious scheme to peg its battered currency to US dollar was suggested by Steve H Hanke, little-known American economist who promoted idea; plan has worked recently in smaller countries like Bulgaria, Lithuania and Argentina, but is dubious remedy for big country whose banking system needs major overhaul; IMF has threatened to cut off aid and Pres Clinton is sending Walter Mondale to talk with Pres Suharto; Hanke offers tart conservative prescriptions in Forbes magazine and is officer of Canadian company that has profited by betting against Asia's falling currencies; he dismisses skeptics, interview; photo (M)

1998-02-28.title: World Court Claims Jurisdiction In Pan Am Flight 103 Case
1998-02-28.abstract: World Court in The Hague rules it has authority to decide whether Libya should surrender two suspects for trial on charges of blowing up Pan American Flight 103 over Lockerbie, Scotland, in 1988; rejects US and British claims to jurisdiction (S)

1998-02-28.title: Mexico Expels French Priest in Latest Move Against Outsiders in Rebel Area
1998-02-28.abstract: Mexican Government deports Father Michel Henri Jean Chanteau, French priest who served for 32 years in Indian parish in state of Chiapas, turning campaign against foreigners in Chiapas into new confrontation with Catholic Church; he is fifth foreign priest, including two Americans, to be deported from Chiapas since Zapatista rebellion erupted in 1994; photo (M)

1998-02-28.title: General Tells Rwanda Court Massacre Was Preventable
1998-02-28.abstract: Maj Gen Romeo Dallaire of Canada, former commander of UN peacekeeping troops in Rwanda, tells UN tribunal that 1994 massacre of at least 500,000 people could have been halted if small, well-armed force had been sent in early with that objective; says his force was too small, ill-equipped and poorly trained, and lacked legal mandate to intervene; unanswered is why UN officials apparently ignored his warning that Rwandan Government was planning massacre and why he was not given new mandate to step in; questions are politically delicate for Sec Gen Kofi Annan, who in 1994 was head of UN peacekeeping operations; documents leaked to reporters last year suggested Dallaire had warned Annan; map (M)

1998-02-28.title: Cambodian Truce Declared, Raising Hopes for New Vote
1998-02-28.abstract: Cambodia's two main rival factions declare cease-fire, raising tentative hopes for peace plan and competitive elections later in year; forces loyal to Second Prime Min Hun Sen and Prince Norodom Ranariddh make separate announcements (S)
